ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wafer food concept; no specific rolled cream-filled wafer roll entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers wafers as a biscuit/cracker subcategory. No specific cream-filled wafer roll code exists; closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for cream-filled wafer roll; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single wafer roll piece weighs approximately 10–12 g; a common snack serving is 3–4 pieces (approximately 33–44 g).

nutrient_source.title: Wafer roll (any flavour) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Wafer roll (any flavour)' (cr_id F11080078). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cream-filled wafer rolls was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 76 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain wafer biscuits / refined wheat-flour sweet biscuits (GI ~70–76) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. The high sugar content (37.5 g/100g) and refined flour base support a high GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (76 × 26.7) / 100 ≈ 20.3 for a 36.6 g serving (3 pieces × ~12 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (37.5 g/100g) combined with moderate sodium (198 mg/100g) and ultra-processed formulation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit wafer rolls, as the very high sugar content (37.5 g/100g) promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and elevates cardiovascular risk, while the 198 mg/100g sodium further compounds blood pressure burden in a typical snack portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar (37.5 g/100g) and high total carbohydrate (72.9 g/100g) with minimal dietary fibre (2.3 g/100g), yielding a high glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Wafer rolls are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the sugar content of 37.5 g/100g far exceeds safe thresholds, total carbohydrate is 72.9 g/100g with only 2.3 g fibre to moderate absorption, and the estimated GI of 76 produces a glycaemic load of approximately 20 per serving, causing rapid and sustained blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (estimated ~8.5 g/100g from palm oil base), very high sugar, and ultra-processed formulation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wafer rolls should be avoided by individuals with cardiovascular disease, as the estimated saturated fat of approximately 8.5 g/100g (from palm-oil-based vegetable fat) raises LDL cholesterol, the very high sugar content (37.5 g/100g) promotes triglyceride elevation and endothelial dysfunction, and the ultra-processed nature of the product is independently associated with increased CVD risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory saturated fat from palm oil, high refined sugar, and likely trace trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid wafer rolls, as the combination of palm-oil-derived saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g), high refined sugar (37.5 g/100g), and potential trace trans fat all activate pro-inflammatory pathways that can worsen joint inflammation and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wafer roll (any flavour) is a crispy, tube-shaped baked snack made from thin sheets of rolled wafer filled with a sweet cream or flavoured filling. The wafer shell is produced from a refined wheat flour batter baked into a thin, crisp sheet that is immediately rolled into a cylinder while still warm; the hollow centre is then filled with a cream mixture typically based on vegetable fat (palm oil), sugar, and flavouring agents such as chocolate, vanilla, strawberry, or pandan. The texture is characteristically light, crunchy, and brittle, with a sweet, fatty cream interior that contrasts with the dry, neutral wafer exterior. Wafer rolls are widely available across Southeast Asia, Europe, and globally as a packaged snack food. From a nutritional standpoint, wafer rolls are an ultra-processed, energy-dense snack providing 470 kcal per 100 g, with a very high carbohydrate content of 72.9 g/100g dominated by sugar at 37.5 g/100g. Fat content is 17.2 g/100g, predominantly saturated fat from palm oil (estimated ~8.5 g/100g). Protein is low at 5.4 g/100g and dietary fibre is minimal at 2.3 g/100g. Sodium is 198 mg/100g. The estimated glycaemic index is 76 (high), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 20 per standard serving. Clinically, wafer rolls are unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to their very high sugar, saturated fat, and ultra-processed composition. They offer no meaningful micronutrient benefit, with calcium and iron reported as 0 mg/100g in the HPB source.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a wafer roll?
Is wafer roll high in sugar?
Is wafer roll suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of wafer roll?
Can I eat wafer rolls on a diet?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080078) with a stated confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is estimated at ~8.5 g/100g based on typical palm-oil-based wafer roll formulations cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central analogous entries, and is documented as an estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (8.5 g/100g) is estimated from typical palm-oil-based wafer roll formulations; the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Wafers, cream-filled' (NDB No. 18433) which reports saturated fat ~8.3–9.0 g/100g. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields beyond calcium and iron are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and micronutrient data for this generic wafer roll are not reported in the HPB Foodo database, and no sufficiently close approved-source entry provides these values with confidence. 3. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 per the HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F11080078). 4. water_g is set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this entry. 5. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat-flour sweet biscuits/wafers from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for cream-filled wafer rolls was located. 6. This food is classified as international/generic; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine-specific multilingual naming requirement applies under G6. Local-language name fields are left null accordingly. 7. The input record listed calcium and iron as 0.0 — these are confirmed zero values per the HPB source and are retained as 0.0 (not -1) in per_100g.
